details.text: Derivative: الْمُطَفِّفِينَ come from طَفّفَ. A مُطَفِّف (Mutaffif) is someone who swindles others by giving a little less than what is rightfully due.

Derivative: وَيْلٌ can be used as a kind of curse with the meaning of: “May destruction or ruin befall someone” or “May the ultimate destruction be upon someone”. In respect to oneself it can carry the meaning of: “Woe upon me, what ruin and destruction has befallen me”.

Derivative: أَثِيمٍ comes from أَثِمَ – إِثْم (Ithm). Ithm is commonly translated as “a sin”. أَثِيمٍ is the مُبالَغَة (accentuated and emphasised) form and means someone who commits a lot of sins and heinous acts. آثِم (Aathim) is someone who sins and أَثِيمٍ (Atheem) is the one who perpetually sins.

Derivative: مُعْتَدٍ from اِعْتَدَى which means to violate or infringe against somebody else’s rights or to cross or transgress against the limits. مُعْتَدٍ is the one who wrongs somebody else or transgresses against and crosses the limits that he is meant to adhere to.
Derivative: تُتْلَىٰ from تَلَا – يَتْلُو – تَلْوًا and تِلَاوَةً. It means to follow right behind something and also means to read something or to read out loud to someone.

Derivative: أَسَاطِيرُ is the plural of three singulars أُسْطُورَة – أسَيْطَرَة – أَسْطَار and its origin is from سَطْر which refers to a line that is written down. It refers to myths and legends that were told as stories and written down. أَسَاطِيرُ are myths and legends which are passed down generation by generation.

Derivative: رَانَ refers to an imprint and impurity that dominates over the heart. It has been said to describe the sin that is perpetrated after sin until the heart turns black. The verb رَانَ means to take hold of and to become dominant over and is used here to depict the hearts being taken over by sin and becoming black as a result.
Derivative: مَحْجُوبُونَ from حِجاب (Hijaab) which is a barrier. مَحْجُوبُونَ means “they will be blocked and screened off by a barrier”.
Derivative: الْأَبْرَارِ means “the righteous” and it is the plural of the few form (جَمْعُ القِلَّة ) because the righteous will be few in comparison.

Derivative: عِلِّيِّينَ comes from عُلُو (‘Uluw) which means height. عِلِّيِّينَ is in the sound masculine plural form which is used for creatures who possess a mind or intellect (عَقْل) and as such refers to those who are the highest or most elevated i.e., the Angels who are or the most elevated level or standing. Another understanding for عِلِّيِّينَ is that it refers to a high and honoured place in the highest part of Jannah. عِلِّيِّينَ has also been said to refer to the name of the book of the Abraar (righteous).
Derivative: يَسْتَوْفُونَ from اِسْتَوْفَى and means that they demand or seek to receive their due in full. It implies attempting to get the most out of something.
Derivative: اكْتَالُوا from اِكْتالَ and it means: They receive a measure or weight of a commodity i.e., a certain quantity.

- The use of اِكْتالَ عَلَى (to take a measure upon or against) in this verse instead of the usual اِكْتالَ مِنْ (to take a measure from) indicates that the receiving or seeking of the due measure in the case being described is being demanded with a level of being overbearing i.e., they are overbearing against people to make sure that they get their full and due measure.
Derivative: الْأَرَائِكِ refers to a bed or couch which is used to recline and relax upon and is adorned or furnished with cushions.
Derivative: نَضْرَةَ refers to the lighting up or radiating of someone’s face, the freshness and joy in it.
Derivative: مَخْتُومٍ means “sealed” and implies that no one has opened or drank from it prior to the one who will receive it in Jannah.
Derivative: رَحِيقٍ has been described as a delicious wine that has been purified from any filth or blemish which doesn’t intoxicate.

Derivative: يَتَنَافَس is from تَنَافَسَ – تَنافُسًا and its meaning in origin is to strive and compete for something precious which is highly valued by the people, in the case where everyone desires it exclusively for themselves.

Derivative: تَسْنِيم comes from سَنَمَة which means a height, summit or peak and سَنَام which refers to the hump (or highest part) of a camel. تَسْنِيم is used to mean to elevate something. It refers to a spring that is in an elevated place which is called Tasneem (تَسْنِيم).
Derivative: مِزَاجُ in origin refers to that which has something mixed with it and here it means to be mixed with something.

عَيْنًا يَشْرَبُ بِهَا الْمُقَرَّبُونَ

- “A spring which those who have been made close (الْمُقَرَّبُونَ) drink by it” – The fact that those who have been made close (الْمُقَرَّبُونَ) have direct access to the spring whilst the الْأَبْرَارَ (Al-Abraar - The righteous) are given a drink with a mixture of it, indicates that الْمُقَرَّبُونَ (Al-Muqarraboon) are of a higher rank and station to الْأَبْرَارَ (Al-Abraar – The righteous).

Derivative: أَجْرَمُوا from أَجْرَمَ means to perpetrate crimes or offenses or engage in criminality. It also captures in its meaning that the consequences of the crime will be seen by its perpetrator.
Derivative: يُخْسِرُونَ – خَسِرَ means to lose or make a loss. يُخْسِرُونَ is from أَخْسَرَ which means to make someone else lose out. Here it means to give less than what is due and deserved.

وَإِذَا كَالُوهُمْ أَوْ وَزَنُوهُمْ يُخْسِرُونَ

- Usually the verbs كَالُوا (they measure) and وَزَنُوا (they weigh) would be followed by a لَام (Laam - ل) to indicate “for” i.e., كَالُوا لَهُمْ (they measure “for” them) and وَزَنُوا لَهُمْ (they weigh “for” them) and this can reflect a rhetorical style in the Arabic where the missing حَرْف (Harf – prepositional letter) of لَام (Laam - ل) implies that the transaction of weighing and measuring being undertaken is missing some of its core elements just like the construction of the sentence is missing something.
Derivative: يَتَغَامَزُونَ means to wink at each other. غَمْز (Ghamz) in Arabic refers to making gestures with your eyes.
Derivative: فَكِهِينَ describes their state and comes from فَكِهَ which means to be gleeful. فَكِهِينَ means that they are in a state of jubilant glee and pleased with themselves.

وَمَا أُرْسِلُوا عَلَيْهِمْ حَافِظِينَ

- This has been interpreted in two different ways. It literally means: “And they had not been sent over them as guardians” i.e., the disbelievers were not sent to watch over the believers. It is like saying that the state of the believers is none of your concern, right or business for you to pass judgement upon them.

Derivative: يَفْعَلُونَ – The difference between يَفْعَلُونَ and يَعْمَلُونَ is that the latter implies doing something consciously and based on thought whilst يَفْعَلُونَ implies doing something without such thought i.e., it refers to a more baseless and thoughtless action.
Derivative: ثُوِّبَ – ثَواب (Thawaab) in Arabic refers to a good recompense for what has been done or for work that has been undertaken. ثُوِّبَ means to be recompensed and rewarded well.
Derivative: سِجِّينٍ – سِجْنٌ (Sijn) in Arabic refers to a prison and سِجِّينٍ (Sijjeen) can refer to a horrible prison using the accentuated or emphasised فَعِيل (Fa’eel) form of the word.
Derivative: كَلَّا means "not at all" i.e., the matter is not how they think or believe.

Derivative: الْفُجَّارِ (Al-Fujjaar) is the plural of فَاجِر (Faajir) and the Faajir is someone who disobeys Allah in the most open and insolent manner, who acts sinfully with pride and perpetrates the worst kinds of crimes.
- “The Book of Al-Fujjaar” refers to a list or register of Al-Fujjaar and a record of their crimes.
Derivative: مَرْقُومٌ means to be written with thick clear writing and to print it so that it cannot be erased. It implies that which will not be forgotten and that which will not be erased.
